ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for teriyaki salmon plate; closest_parent 'salmon food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Salmon, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific teriyaki-glazed salmon plate code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for teriyaki salmon plate as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (salmon fillet with rice and accompaniments)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard teriyaki salmon plate typically consists of a grilled salmon fillet (approximately 120–150 g) served over or alongside steamed Japanese short-grain rice (approximately 150–180 g), totalling roughly 350 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15237) combined with teriyaki sauce nutrient contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for the salmon component are drawn from USDA SR Legacy entry for Atlantic farmed salmon cooked dry heat. Teriyaki glaze contribution (soy sauce, mirin, sugar) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods entries for soy sauce and sugar. Steamed rice component uses USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked white short-grain rice. Final per-100g values represent a weighted composite of the full plate (salmon ~40%, rice ~46%, sauce ~14% by weight), consistent with a 350 g serving. This is an adapted/estimated composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI ~54–56; teriyaki salmon plate GI estimated from rice-dominant component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a teriyaki salmon plate as a composite dish. The GI of 54 is estimated from the dominant glycemic component: Japanese short-grain cooked white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 54–56 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). The salmon and teriyaki glaze components contribute minimal additional glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 57.75) / 100 ≈ 31.2, rounded to 31.0. Serving carbohydrate estimated at 16.5 g/100g × 350 g = 57.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from teriyaki soy sauce glaze
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the teriyaki glaze contributes approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,330 mg per full plate serving), which represents over 55% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce or a lighter glaze application is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from white rice component and added sugar in teriyaki glaze
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should note that the white rice component contributes to a low GI of approximately 54 and a glycemic load of approximately 31 per serving; while this is a lower GI option, portion control of rice and pairing with the high-protein salmon component remain important for managing postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon offset by moderate sodium from teriyaki sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; however, the sodium content from the teriyaki glaze should be monitored, and individuals with cardiovascular disease are encouraged to choose reduced-sodium preparations and consume this dish as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teriyaki Salmon Plate is a Japanese dish consisting of a grilled salmon fillet coated in a sweet and savoury teriyaki glaze, typically served alongside or over steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The teriyaki glaze is made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, which is reduced to a shiny, caramelised coating on the fish during grilling. The salmon is tender and flaky with a lightly charred exterior, delivering a rich umami flavour balanced by the sweetness of the glaze. This dish is widely served in Japanese restaurants and casual dining chains across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, the salmon component provides high-quality protein (approximately 11.8 g per 100 g of the composite plate) and is an excellent source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin D, vitamin B12, and selenium. The white rice component contributes the majority of the carbohydrate content, resulting in a low glycemic index of approximately 54. The teriyaki sauce adds meaningful sodium (approximately 380 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarians, though it contains soy and gluten allergens from the soy sauce component. It is a nutritionally balanced meal option when consumed in standard portions, offering a favourable macronutrient profile with moderate energy density of approximately 148 kcal per 100 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in teriyaki salmon plate?
Is teriyaki salmon good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked Atlantic farmed salmon, cooked white short-grain rice, and soy sauce/sugar components. No exact HPB Singapore or USDA Branded Food entry exists for this specific teriyaki salmon plate as served at Sukiya Singapore. The composite estimation methodology is defensible and transparent, warranting medium confidence.
notes: Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of the full plate estimated at 350 g (salmon fillet ~140 g / 40%, steamed rice ~161 g / 46%, teriyaki sauce ~49 g / 14%). Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed negligible in cooked salmon and soy-based sauce per USDA FoodData Central). GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant glycemic component (Japanese short-grain white rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries and are approximate. Halal status is marked false as mirin and sake (rice wine) are alcoholic ingredients used in traditional teriyaki preparation; halal-certified variants may substitute these. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') appear to be platform-level restaurant tags for Sukiya and are not relevant to this specific dish; they have been disregarded. Food ID GD000964 preserved from input.
